coredata.dc:title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from chelating agent and used as fluorescent probes for accurate detection of ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ)
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Guiqiao
Zhang, Shurong
Cui, Jinzhi
Gao, Wensu
Rong, Xing
Lu, Yaxin
Gao, Canzhu
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel ionic sensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) was developed for sensitive detection of hypochlorite ions (ClO−) and dichromate ions (Cr(Ⅵ)) by fluorescence spectrometry. The N-CQDs was synthesized by hydrothermal method using Methyl Glycine Diacetic acid Trisodium Salt (MGDA) and Ethylenediamine (EDA) with bright blue fluorescence, high fluorescence quantum yield, abundant surface groups and good dispersion. The N-CQDs had a remarkable emission characteristic at 450 nm under the ultraviolet light of 350 nm, and the ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ) ions could quantificationally quench the fluorescence of this emission band. The results showed that N-CQDs had broad linear detection range and the detection limits of trace ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ) ions are 5.0 μM and 2.1 μM, respectively. Subsequently, further verify the reliability of this study and the N-CQDs played an excellent role in the ion detection of actual water samples. The quenching mechanism of ClO− is proved to be dominated by static quenching, while the quenching mechanism of Cr(Ⅵ) is mainly due to inner filter effect. This study is envisioned to efficiently prepare N-CQDs with novel raw materials, provide enlightening insights for enriching the detection of various trace ions by CQDs, and open up a new way to use fluorescence characteristics for water detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Hypochlorite ions
Dichromate ions
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing of ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ)
Fluorescence detection of ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ)
Detection performance of ions in natural water

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Thanks for the support of experimental Instrument Platform of School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shandong University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM, HRTEM (the top inset) and the size distribution (the bottom inset) for N-CQDs. (b) FTIR spectrum of MGDA and N -CQDs samples. (c) XPS full-survey and high-resolution XPS of (d) C1s, (e) N1s and (f) O1s for N-CQDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption and the fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs. Inset: the pictures of N-CQDs under the sunlight and UV light (365 nm); (b) Excitation-emission matrix for N-CQDs; The stability of fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs in different pH (c) and salt solutions (d).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs at room temperature in various concentrations of ClO−. (b) Relationship between the FQE of N-CQDs and the concentration of ClO− (0–3000 μM); inset: the linear relationship between FQE and the concentration of ClO− (0–300 μM). (c) The fluorescent intensity changes of N-CQDs and N-CQDs + ClO− in the presence of different ions.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs at room temperature in various concentrations of Cr(Ⅵ). (b) Relationship between the FQE of N-CQDs and the concentration of Cr(Ⅵ) (0–500 μM); inset: the linear relationship between FQE and the concentration of Cr(Ⅵ) (0–100 μM). (c) The fluorescent intensity changes of N-CQDs and N-CQDs + Cr(Ⅵ) in the presence of different ions.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N-CQDs, N-CQDs + ClO− and CQDs + Cr(Ⅵ). (b) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of ClO−, Cr(Ⅵ) and the fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs. (c) Fluorescence decay traces of N-CQDs, N-CQDs + ClO- and CQDs + Cr(Ⅵ).
Comparison with other response systems for detecting ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ).
Quantitative determination of ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ) in actual water samples.

head.ce:title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from chelating agent and used as fluorescent probes for accurate detection of ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Qingdao, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Qingdao, Shandong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel ionic sensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) was developed for sensitive detection of hypochlorite ions (ClO−) and dichromate ions (Cr(Ⅵ)) by fluorescence spectrometry. The N-CQDs was synthesized by hydrothermal method using Methyl Glycine Diacetic acid Trisodium Salt (MGDA) and Ethylenediamine (EDA) with bright blue fluorescence, high fluorescence quantum yield, abundant surface groups and good dispersion. The N-CQDs had a remarkable emission characteristic at 450 nm under the ultraviolet light of 350 nm, and the ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ) ions could quantificationally quench the fluorescence of this emission band. The results showed that N-CQDs had broad linear detection range and the detection limits of trace ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ) ions are 5.0 μM and 2.1 μM, respectively. Subsequently, further verify the reliability of this study and the N-CQDs played an excellent role in the ion detection of actual water samples. The quenching mechanism of ClO− is proved to be dominated by static quenching, while the quenching mechanism of Cr(Ⅵ) is mainly due to inner filter effect. This study is envisioned to efficiently prepare N-CQDs with novel raw materials, provide enlightening insights for enriching the detection of various trace ions by CQDs, and open up a new way to use fluorescence characteristics for water detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Compared with usual carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have many significant advantages: a comprehensive source of raw materials and low price, good biocompatibility, less environmental pollution, easy functionalization of surface groups, excellent fluorescence characteristics, etc. [1–6]. These extraordinary properties promote its applications in biological imaging [7,8], analytical detection [9,10], optoelectronic devices [11,12] and photocatalysis [13–15]. For the past few years, it has been proved that heteroatom doping [16] can effectively improve the fluorescence performance of CQDs, such as nitrogen [17], boron [18], sulfur [19], phosphorus [20], manganese [21] or other elements [22,23], which can meet the requirements of CQDs in biological imaging [23–25], fluorescence detection [20,26] and environmental protection [27–30]. Nitrogen has a similar size and structure to carbon, and its lone pair electrons easily bond to carbon-based materials [8,31], significantly improving the optical properties of CQDs. Hence, the nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) are superior synthesis strategy than others.

It is reported that the synthesis strategy of N-CQDs is mainly based on nitriding of CQDs or fusion of precursors. The former is always prepared CQDs and then doped with nitrogen or other passivators. The latter approach is usually a bottom-up approach involving reactions between precursors followed by dehydration, polymerization (or aggregation) [31]. In contrast, the latter method is preferred because of mild reaction conditions, high yield and easy introduction of heteroatoms into CQDs [32]. Thereinto, the hydrothermal method is considered to be the most common and convenient system for synthesizing CQDs, which gets excellent application value. Therefore, appropriate preparation strategies and cost-effective precursors still have great scientific significance for the research of N-CQDs.

Nowadays, environmental problems seriously threaten the normal life of human beings, including all kinds of materials flooding and serious exceeding the standard. Hypochlorite (ClO−) is a kind of crucial reactive oxygen species, which has strong oxidation capacity and bleaching capacity [33] and has been widely used in the bleaching and water disinfection process. It is convinced that ClO− in an appropriate amount can play an effective anti-inflammatory and bactericidal role in organisms, inhibiting the infiltration of bacteria in the immune system [34,35]. However, an excess of ClO− will lead to cardiovascular diseases, arthritis and even cancer, seriously threatening health [33]. Heavy metals are severe hazard to the environment and human health, among which dichromate (Cr(Ⅵ)) is a high pollutant in the ecosystem [36]. Low concentrations of Cr(Ⅵ) can cause ulcers and other injuries, while high concentrations can directly damage the kidneys, liver and even death [37]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop simple, sensitive, and selective methods for detecting these ions and making an early warning in time.

Herein, we developed an efficient method for rapid and sensitive detection of ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ) ions with the high fluorescence properties of N-CQDs. Using an environmentally friendly chelating agent Methyl Glycine Diacetic acid Trisodium Salt (MGDA) [38–40] and Ethylenediamine (EDA) as precursors, we prepared fluorescent N-CQDs using a one-step hydrothermal technique. The product has bright blue fluorescence, which can be used as an efficient fluorescent sensor to detect ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ) ions. This method has a fast response time, low detection limit, and achieves rapid detection in natural water samples.

MGDA (purity = 40%) and EDA were purchased from Shandong Taihe Water Treatment Technologies Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). HCl, NaOH, HNO3, H2SO4, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, KCl, BaCl2·2H2O, NaH2PO4·2H2O, Na2HPO4·12H2O, NaHCO3, NH4Cl, HClO and K2Cr2O7 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). CuCl2·2H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, MnSO4·H2O, CdCl2·2.5H2O, NiSO4·6H2O, AgNO3, AlCl3, ZnCl2, KBr and KI were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the reagents and solvents of analytical grades used without further purification. All the experiments were carried out using deionized water.

The N-CQDs were prepared by the typical one-step hydrothermal method. In short, MGDA (0.0054 mol) and EDA (0.0108 mol) were dispersed in deionized water (the molar ratio of MGDA to EDA = 1:2). After mixing well, adjust the pH to about 5.0 and the volume to 30 mL, and then transfer the mixture into an 80 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave to undergo a hydrothermal treatment at 220 °C for 6 h. After cooling naturally to room temperature (about 22 °C), the dark brown solution was passed through a 0.22 μm filter. The obtained filter liquor ate dialysis in deionized water (MW: 500 Da) for 24 h to remove small molecules formed in the hydrothermal process. Finally, the N-CQDs was dried and refrigerated at 4 °C.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of N-CQDs were examined under JEOL JEM-2100F (Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The N-CQDs’ X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectra were collected on a Thermo K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (America). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) characteristics of MGDA and N-CQDS in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 and the resolution of 4 cm−1 were analyzed on Thermo Nicolet IS50 Micro-Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (America). The fluorescence lifetime was measured using EI fls1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Britain). The 1H NMR spectra of N-CQDs and related substances (solvent: D2O) were characterized by the Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Spectrometer (Bruker Avance NEO 600, 400 MHz, Switzerland). The UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs was obtained on UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), and the fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs samples were collected on an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) in a standard quartz cell with lid, respectively. All fluorescence spectrophotometers have the same parameter settings: scanning speed (1200 nm min−1), excitation bandwidth (5 nm), emission bandwidth (5 nm), the voltage of the photomultiplier tube (700 V).

In this project, the relative QY was calculated by using quinine sulfate solution (QY is 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) as a reference and based on the research methods in published studies [17,41]. The calculation formula is as follows:
(1)
Q
Y S
= Q
Y R
×
(
I S
/
I R
)
×
(
A R
/
A S
)
×
(
η S
/
η R
)
2

In the above equation, QY represents the fluorescence quantum yield, I represents the integral area of fluorescence intensity, A is the absorbance values and η is the refraction coefficient of the solvent (generally regarded as 1.0). The subscripts S and R denote N-CQDS and quinine sulfate solution, respectively.

To evaluate the fluorescence properties of N-CQDs and various added ions, the 20 mg L−1 N-CQDs solution was used as the reserve solution. At the same time, the aqueous solutions of common ions were prepared, including Fe3+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cr2O7 2−, ClO−, Cl−, I−, Br−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, HCO3 −, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, PO4 3− and H2O2. When an equal number of ions were added to the N-CQDs solution, it was found that only dichromate and hypochlorite had apparent quenching phenomenon. Thus, the N-CQDs solution was sensitive to the dichromate and hypochlorite. With this sensing property the fluorescence emission and other characteristics were studied. Then, the ClO− ions and other ions were added into the N-CQDs solution to investigate the quenching phenomenon and sensing characteristics under ion interference. The same experimental process with Cr2O7 2− ions.

Three water samples including tap water, river water and seawater were collected from Shandong University (Qingdao, Shandong Province). All the water samples collected were filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to remove impurities and diluted with buffer solution to maintain acidity balance. Then adjusted the content in the water samples by adding a quantitative ClO− or Cr(Ⅵ) and carried out fluorescence test for verification.

In the early stage of the study, the temperature, time, pH and the ratio of reactants affecting the formation of carbon quantum dots were discussed in detail. As shown in Fig. S1, the optimum temperature was discussed, and the reasons for the phenomena may be that appropriate temperature rise and reaction time were conducive to the improvement of the degree of carbonization, while too high temperature or too long time may lead to the oxidation and agglomeration of quantum dots, thus reducing the yield. In addition, it was found that acidic environment and appropriate precursor ratio were beneficial to improve the fluorescence intensity, possibly because of the increase of chromophore content in the product. Therefore, under the same amount of carbon source, the optimal reaction conditions were determined to be 220 °C, 6 h, pH = 5, and the molar ratio of raw materials was 1:2. Under these conditions, the fluorescence quantum yield was calculated as 22.14% (Eq. (1)).

The morphology of N-CQDs was observed and characterized by TEM. As can be seen from Fig. 1 a, the N-CQDs has an exceptional spherical shape and good dispersion. About 100 dots were randomly selected for particle size statistics. It can be seen from the bottom inset (Fig. 1a) that the particle size of N-CQDs is between 3.5 nm and 7 nm, with an average particle size of 5.39 nm, which conforms to the general characteristics [9,11] of carbon quantum dots. The HRTEM image in Fig. 1a (the top inset) reveals that the N-CQDs has a distinct crystal structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [22].

The surface functional groups of precursor and product were compared by FT-IR spectra. As represented in Fig. 1b, there are many similar peaks in the infrared spectra of the MGDA and the N-CQDs, which indicates that the reactive groups in the precursor had been successfully transferred into the N-CQDs. The absorption peak at 3358 cm−1 is broader than that of MGDA, which may be due to the stretching vibration of N–H and O–H [42]. The peak at 2921 cm−1 corresponds to C–H bonds stretching vibration [32]. The characteristic peaks at 1617 cm−1 and 1385 cm−1 may be attributed to the stretching vibrations of conjugated structure CO and CC bonds [43]. The two peaks at 1534 cm−1 and 1211 cm−1 correspond to the bending vibration of N–H bonds and the tensile vibration of C–N bonds [26], respectively, indicating that the nitrogen element in EDA has been doped into the N-CQDs. Furthermore, the 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S2) reveals the peaks at 1.375 ppm and 3.283 ppm, which are attributed to methyl in MGDA and methylene in ethylenediamine. The 1H NMR of N-CQDs (Fig. S3) shows abundant new peaks on the basis of retaining the original methyl and methylene groups, indicating the complexity and diversification of its hydrogen-containing groups. Therefore, it can be known that the N-CQDs has abundant polar groups, making them have strong hydrophilicity, thus enhancing their stability and chemical sensing ability in solution.

The XPS spectrum used to characterize the chemical states and composition of the N-CQDs. The survey scan of XPS (Fig. 1c) reveals distinct peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.8 eV and 530.8 eV corresponding to C1s (63.91%), N1s (14.11%) and O1s (21.3%) electrons, respectively [42]. The core region of C1s (Fig. 1d) shows five fitted peaks exhibited at 288.4 eV, 287.7 eV, 286.1 eV, 285.6 eV and 284.8 eV, corresponding to the O–C–O, CO, C–O, C–N, and C–C (sp3)/CC (sp2) bonds [14], respectively. Then, appeared in N1s high-resolution spectra of the N-CQDs (Fig. 1e) are three peaks at 401.4 eV, 400.6 eV and 399.4 eV, which would belong to the N–H, N-(C3) and C–N bonds [1,6]. In addition, two sub-peaks are observed for the XPS of O1s (Fig. 1f), in which the peaks at 532.1 eV and 530.8 eV, which are assigned to the oxygen associated to the bonds of C–OH/C–O–C and CO bonds [43], respectively. Therefore, analysis of the significant elemental composition and the functionalized groups implies that the product are nitrogen doped CQDs with multiple surface bonds, consistent with the FT-IR and 1H NMR analysis.

The optical properties of N-CQDs were characterized by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the weak acromion at 240 nm could be attributed to the π–π∗ transition of the conjugated CC bonds [44]. The broad absorption band between 290 nm and 350 nm, which would be attributed to the n–π∗ transition of the CO bonds and the CN bonds [45,46]. The photographs inserted in Fig. 2a shows that the N-CQDs solution appears pale yellow under sunlight and has a distinct blue fluorescence under an ultraviolet lamp. In addition, there are apparent excitation and emission peaks at 350 nm and 440 nm (Fig. 2a, blue and red line), which are consistent with the excitation-emission matrix for N-CQDs in Fig. 2b. Fig. 2b further shows that the emission spectrum of N-CQDs is not depend by the change of excitation wavelength. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of N-CQDs is consistently maintained at 440 nm when the excitation wavelength increases from 200 nm to 400 nm, which may be due to the influence of surface states on the energy band-gap.

The photostability tests were performed to evaluate the feasibility of N-CQDs in natural environments. As shown in Fig. 2c, the N-CQDs has relatively stable optical properties in acidic environment, while their PL intensity decreases in alkaline solution, which may be caused by the destruction of surface hydroxyl functional groups in alkaline environment. Therefore, in subsequent detection experiments, phosphoric acid buffer solution was used as the solvent to eliminate the influence of pH changes. The PL intensity of N-CQDs remains extremely stable at different concentrations of NaCl (0–1 mM) (Fig. 2d), which means that N-CQDs has good salt tolerance and excellent fluorescence performance in salt environment. Subsequently, it is found that there is a remarkable linear relationship between the concentration of N-CQDs and the PL intensity (Fig. S4), indicating its stability and reliability of the fluorescence characteristics, which demonstrates the great application potential of the proposed N-CQDs in the natural environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ)
ce:section.ce:para: The PL intensity of N-CQDs is extremely sensitive to some ionic disturbances [47], so it is the best candidate for detecting some substances. Therefore, an equal number of multiple ions were added to the N-CQDs solution to monitor the change of PL intensity (Fig. S5). Different ions (Fe3+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Cl−, I−, Br−, HCO3 −, H2O2, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, PO4 3−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, Cr2O7 2− and ClO−, 5 mM) were added to the N-CQDs solutions (20 mg L−1). The results show that the PL intensity of N-CQDs solution is decreased obviously only under the interference of Cr(Ⅵ) or ClO−. Therefore, the N-CQDs solution is a superior fluorescent probe for Cr(Ⅵ) or ClO− detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ)
ce:section.ce:para:
To demonstrate the accuracy of N-CQDs in detecting hypochlorite ions, the ClO− of different concentrations were added to the N-CQDs solution. As shown in Fig. 3 a and Fig. 3b, the PL intensity of the N-CQDs solution was quenched continuously while increasing the concentration of ClO− (0–3000 μM). It can be discovered from the inset of Fig. 3b that there is a good linear relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency (FQE = 1 – F/F
0 ) and ion concentration (0–300 μM), and the linear equation can be fitted as: y = 0.0017C - 0.0039 (R2 = 0.9979). The limit of detection (LOD) could be estimated to be 5.0 μM by the detection limit calculation formula according to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) (Eq. (2)) [48,49].
(2)
L O D = K S / m

In the above equation, S represents the standard deviation of blank and 10 blank values were used as the standard deviation calculation basis, K is a constant related to degree of confidence (K = 3), m represents the slope value of the regression line.

Subsequently, various interfering ions were added to the N-CQDs + ClO− solution again to evaluate the quenching situation (Fig. 3c). The results show that the addition of excessive interfering ions had no effect on fluorescence quenching, which provides a prerequisite for the detection of ClO− in natural water samples.

In the same way, the accuracy of the detection of Cr(Ⅵ) by N-CQDs was verified. Fig. 4 a and Fig. 4b showed the fluorescence emission intensity of N-CQDs under the influence of different concentrations of Cr(Ⅵ). With the increase of concentration (0–500 μM), the PL intensity of N-CQDs solution decreases, and the linear equation between FQE and ion concentration (0–100 μM) fitted well: y = 0.0041C-0.0195 (R2 = 0.9922). The LOD is estimated to be 2.1 μM by the formula (Eq. (2)). Subsequently, the anti-interference performance of N-CQDs in Cr(Ⅵ) detection was analyzed, as shown in Fig. 4c, which suggests that the incorporation of ions does not affect the detection process and will certainly facilitate their application in natural water systems. Fig. S6 shows the time response of detection and the system can achieve rapid detection of samples and has excellent stability. Corresponding systems for the detection of ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ) in different reference studies are listed in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection performance of ions in natural water
ce:section.ce:para: An experiment was carried out to detect two kinds of ions in actual water samples. In this study, the concentration of ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ) in water samples was lower than the detection limit. Therefore, quantitative ions were added to the water for recycling experiment. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery rate at different concentration levels are kept between 96% and 107%, with relative standard deviation (RSD) lower than 3%, indicating that N-CQDs probe can be applied to detect ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ) in natural water samples.

This study uses the quenching effect of ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ) ions on carbon quantum dots to achieve quantitative detection. Dynamic quenching refers to the fluorescent substance in the solution and quenching agent collide to produce energy losses [36,59], resulting in decreased of fluorescence intensity, with the most remarkable characteristic is that the fluorescence life of luminescent material is shortened obviously. In contrast, static quenching is due to change of the surface groups’ structure and results in the decrease of the fluorescence intensity. In addition, when the absorption spectrum of quench agent overlaps with the excitation or emission spectrum of carbon quantum dots in the detection system, the inner filter effect (IFE) will occur [60]. Neither static quenching nor IFE resulted in a reduction in fluorescence lifetime. As shown in Fig. 5 a below, the absorption peak of N-CQDs changes significantly after ClO− ions are added, which is embodied in the disappearance of the peak at 350 nm and the appearance of the absorption band at 250 nm [47], indicating that the changes of bonds occurred during quenching and belongs to the characteristics of static quenching. The 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. S7) illustrates that there are obvious differences between N-CQDs and N-CQDs + ClO−, which is reflected in the change of the proportion of some hydrogen-containing functional groups. Compared with Fig. S3, the proton signals at 1.967 ppm and 8.416 ppm is significantly increased, indicating that the carboxyl content of surface groups was increased during quenching [61,62], which was consistent with the characteristics of static quenching described above. However, after adding Cr(Ⅵ) ions into the N-CQDs solution, it was found that the UV–vis absorption spectrum (the red line in Fig. 5a) and 1H NMR (Fig. S8) did not show significant changes, indicating that the incorporation of chromium ions basically does not change the surface structure of N-CQDs, excluding the effect of static quenching.

As shown in Fig. 5b, the absorption peak of Cr(Ⅵ) at 353 nm overlaps with the excitation wavelength of N-CQDs at 350 nm. Thus, the excitation wavelength of N-CQDs would be absorbed by Cr(Ⅵ), resulting in a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity, which suggests that the IFE is a possible mechanism causing fluorescence quenching. On the contrary, the absorption spectra of ClO− does not repeat significantly with the N-CQDs, excluding the role of IFE.

To prove these suppositions, we studied the fluorescence attenuation trend of the three solutions. As shown in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence lifetimes of the three were calculated to be 6.9475 ns, 8.7135 ns and 8.4023 ns, respectively, with no noticeable smaller. This phenomenon indicates that the quenching process does not include the factor of dynamic quenching, indicating that the previous speculations of fluorescence quenching mechanisms are roughly correct.

This study proposed one-step hydrothermal preparation strategy of N-CQDs with two kinds of ion detection effects. The N-CQDs exhibited high fluorescence quantum yield (22.14%), excellent hydrophilicity and salt tolerance. Importantly, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs can be quenched quantitatively by ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ) ions. The N-CQDs has a wide detection field of ClO− and Cr(Ⅵ) ions, with good linearity in the range of 0–300 μM and 0–100 μM, respectively. Moreover, the N-CQDs has been successfully applied to the determination of ions in actual water samples, indicating that N-CQDS has a good application prospect.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Guiqiao Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Shurong Zhang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology. Jinzhi Cui: Writing – review & editing. Wensu Gao: Writing – review & editing. Xing Rong: Writing – review & editing. Yaxin Lu: Writing – review & editing. Canzhu Gao: Project administration, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thanks for the support of experimental Instrument Platform of School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shandong University.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2022.339478.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal treatment of red lentils for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots and its application for sensing Fe3+
Opt. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.M.S.H. Khan, R.S. Rahman, Shumaila, S. Islam, M. Zulfequar, Hydrothermal treatment of red lentils for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots and its application for sensing Fe3+, Optical Materials, 91 (2019) 386-395.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: N-doped carbon quantum dots from osmanthus fragrans as a novel off-on fluorescent nanosensor for highly sensitive detection of quercetin and aluminium ion, and cell imaging
J. Pharmaceut. Biomed. Anal.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Yu, D. Qin, X. Jiang, X. Zheng, B. Deng, N-doped carbon quantum dots from osmanthus fragrans as a novel off-on fluorescent nanosensor for highly sensitive detection of quercetin and aluminium ion, and cell imaging, Journal of pharmaceutical and biomedical analysis, 192 (2021) 113673.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive electrochemical sensor based on xylan-based Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposite for dopamine detection
Appl. Surf. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Han, J. Cai, C. Liu, J. Ren, X. Wang, J. Yang, X. Wang, Highly sensitive electrochemical sensor based on xylan-based Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposite for dopamine detection, Applied Surface Science, 541 (2021) 148566.
